---
title: "Step 3: Implement the Metropolis Update"
---

This step is the conceptual center of the lecture.

## Purpose

Implement one Metropolis update step and use AI to understand a new algorithm through dialogue.

## Target Outcome

By the end of this step, you should have:

- a working Metropolis update
- a basic simulation loop
- a verbal explanation of why the acceptance rule looks the way it does
- a rough understanding of how this differs from heat-bath updates

## Why This Step Matters

The Metropolis method is a good example of what AI should help you do.

The main value is not that AI can generate the code quickly. The main value is that AI can help you understand:

- what the state of the system is
- what one update attempt changes
- why we compute an energy difference `ΔE`
- why lower-energy proposals are always accepted
- why higher-energy proposals are accepted only probabilistically
- which part of the code is the core algorithmic idea

## Prompt to Give the AI

```text
I am learning the Metropolis update for the 2D Ising model in Julia.

Before writing code, explain the algorithm in a beginner-friendly way.
Please distinguish clearly between:

- the state of the system
- what one update attempt does
- why we compute the energy difference ΔE
- why lower-energy moves are accepted immediately
- why higher-energy moves are accepted with probability exp(-beta * ΔE)
- which part of the code is the core of the algorithm

Then compare the Metropolis method with the heat-bath method.

Please explain:
- what Metropolis proposes
- what it accepts or rejects
- what heat-bath samples directly
- how the code structure would differ
- what both methods are trying to achieve

Keep the math light. Focus on intuition and algorithmic meaning.
After that, give me one small implementation step at a time instead of a full solution at once.
```

## Concepts to Acquire

- proposal and acceptance
- energy difference `ΔE`
- one Monte Carlo sweep
- probabilistic acceptance
- Metropolis vs heat-bath

## A Minimal Version

```julia
function metropolis_step!(spins, beta)
    L = size(spins, 1)
    for _ in 1:(L * L)
        i = rand(1:L)
        j = rand(1:L)
        nn = spins[mod1(i + 1, L), j] + spins[mod1(i - 1, L), j] +
             spins[i, mod1(j + 1, L)] + spins[i, mod1(j - 1, L)]
        dE = 2 * spins[i, j] * nn
        if dE <= 0 || rand() < exp(-beta * dE)
            spins[i, j] *= -1
        end
    end
end
```

## Compare Update Rules

Use AI to explain the difference between these two ideas:

- `Metropolis`: propose a flip, then accept or reject it
- `Heat-bath`: sample the new spin directly from the local conditional distribution

You do not need to fully implement both methods in this lecture. You do need to understand the difference in update logic.

## Code-Reading Check

Read this fragment:

```julia
dE = 2 * spins[i, j] * nn
if dE <= 0 || rand() < exp(-beta * dE)
    spins[i, j] *= -1
end
```

Answer:

1. What is `dE` describing?
2. Why are some moves accepted with probability less than 1?
3. Which line contains the essential Metropolis acceptance logic?

## Extra Test Generation Prompt

```text
Create 3 short code-reading questions about the Metropolis method for the 2D Ising model.

Conditions:
- 1 question should ask about the meaning of the algorithm
- 1 question should ask about the acceptance rule
- 1 question should ask about a likely bug or improvement in the code
- do not give the answers yet
- after I answer, grade my responses and give me 1 follow-up question

Then create 2 additional short conceptual questions comparing Metropolis and heat-bath updates.
Focus on the difference in update logic, not long calculations.
```

## Skip Condition

You may skip this step if you can already:

- explain the Metropolis update in words
- identify the acceptance rule in code
- explain the main difference between proposal-and-rejection and direct conditional sampling
